reviews.review_text: game crashes every 15 to 20 minutes and it always crashes when you get to s certain mission and many people are reporting the same thing

One of the videogames of my adolescence and honestly one of the games of my life.

I absolutely love the atmosphere of this game. Paradise islands where at first you feel like the prey trying to survive… but little by little you become the predator. Experiencing that transformation alongside Jason is unforgettable.

The gunplay, hunting, exploration, driving, wingsuit, outpost liberations, getting lost searching for relics… this game was WAY ahead of its time in terms of gameplay. Even today it still feels smooth, immersive and incredibly fun. It has aged beautifully and still looks stunning.

And then there’s Vaas. Simply one of the greatest villains in videogame history.

One detail I’ll always love is hearing innocent songs like “Ukulele Girl” on the radio while driving through one of the most hostile and violent environments imaginable. That contrast between paradise and madness gives Far Cry 3 such a unique identity and atmosphere.

Very few games manage to make you feel how the island slowly consumes and seduces Jason at the same time. It’s hypnotic.

The best part is that nostalgia didn’t blind me. Replaying it years later only made me appreciate it even more.

Historical masterpiece.

TL:DR    "ABSOLUTE CINEMA"

Not a bad game. Story: 8.5/10, gameplay: 9/10. The graphics were top-tier for their time, but they don't really wow you today, so 7/10. Overall, it was quite an unusual game for its time, but it feels a bit dated now, which lowers the final score. On the whole, I recommend this game to anyone looking for a solid action title with a decent story. My overall rating is 7.5/10.

FC3 is undoubtedly one of the most fun games I've ever played. This game was a pioneer in several types of mechanics that are seen in many games today: hiding, running away, hunting—all the actions performed in the game are very dynamic. The story is well-crafted and interesting in the form of missions (the mission to plant the green stuff is really fun) and has a fun ending. Apart from its two fights against the game's villains, which for me are a little weak, the rest of the game's confrontations are exciting and captivating.

This will be heartbreaking to write but I'm not going to do a crazy in-depth review as I wish I could give this a positive review in general. I genuinely ADORE this game and think its mechanics, story and worldbuilding as well as environment and quests are amazing and this WOULD be getting a positive review if it wasn't for how horribly atrocious Ubislop destroyed this game in porting. An absolute monstrosity of a mess to get working on modern hardware. I tried so many fixes ranging from every type of compatibility mode, verifying files, moving to different drives, running as admin with a resolution fix and it took me a few days messing with it just to get the damn game OPEN.
First it'd open in a small black window and nothing just completely frozen, then it'd CTD when changing compatibilities, SOMETIMES IT'D RANDOMELY WORK JUST TO START CRASHING AGAIN! There isn't even a direct way when launching through steam to either use the OG FarCry3.exe or the d3d11 exe. The problem with this is the D11 exe most of the time is always broken. I've played games from 1999 and early 2002 that have needed less hassle to play and setup on modern hardware. This absolutely dogshyt port of a game is highly unstable which is a shame as its such a masterpiece of a game but most people will never be able to see it due to the level of hoops you must jump through just to get it working. Couple this with the UBISLOP launcher asking me to re-enter my password EVERY OTHER TIME SPECIFICALLY FOR THIS GAME?! WHY? 
Anyway here's how I got it working
-Put both exe's in compatibility mode for Windows 7(Sometimes optional if you're experiencing weird visual glitches when doing this but if its crashing a must) 
-Make sure the game always runs as Administrator
-YOU MUST DIRECTLY MODIFY BOTH EXE'S(The normal and Directx11) TO BE 4G LAA Compatible!!(Large Address Aware) This was what ultimately enabled me to finally launch the game and actually play for a long time
-Disable Ubislop overlay, and close background applications such as (MSI Afterburner, Wallpaper Manager) and any other software you don't need necessarily running(This is sometimes optional but stability may vary)
-You must set custom launch options as Steam may try to launch the FarCry3 Directx11 exe which in my experience almost always crashes, sometimes this is due to UbisoftConnect, other times its due to the resolution trying to adapt to your hardware and CTD'ing thus not even letting you fix it in the options menu. Here's an example "G:\SteamLibrary\steamapps\common\Far Cry 3\bin\FarCry3.exe -uplay_steam_mode." You can attempt to use the Direct11 exe after making it LAA but you will possibly experience crashing after longer sessions.
Finally you must pray that the game decides to stay stable and not randomly decide to stop working at will from you doing 🙏  ABSOLUTELY NOTHING!!!!!!!!!! Fix this garbage a.s.s port so I can give this amazing masterpiece a good review cuz currently for the average player or non-technically inclined ain't no way can I see many peeps putting up with this bullish and its only going to get worse on more advanced hardware so it may become wholly unplayable in-general. This is the most unfortunate negative review I have to leave as I cannot leave a positive one when it put me through and is still putting me through hell just to run. I dread when I finish this game uninstall and and decide to come back in a few years, prob gonna become like Jason myself tryna get it functional again an that's IF its still not fixed or there's not some remaster.

Far Cry 3 itself is still an amazing game. The story, atmosphere, characters, and gameplay hold up really well even today. My problem is not with the game — it’s with Ubisoft Connect.

The launcher constantly causes issues and crashes during gameplay. Sometimes the game randomly freezes, crashes to desktop, or refuses to launch properly because of Ubisoft Connect running in the background. It turns what should be a smooth experience into a frustrating one.

It’s disappointing because the actual game is great, but the launcher and bugs seriously hurt the experience. A classic game like this deserves better support and stability.

I have to give it Admistrator permisson. Then, I have to change my password again each time since you have to log in with your password before you play. 

To summarize my frustration, Far cry 3 is a really good game. One of the best I dare say, however the publisher Ubisoft, Is nothing but lazy, incompetent, and a sloppy system. Also, Co Op is not available so some side missions can't be played.

[h1][b]Neither here nor there:[/b] Far Cry 3[/h1]

While, it terms of sales, Far Cry 2 did slightly better than its predecessor, the mixed reviews from critics and players alike were enough to make Ubisoft take a step back and seriously reconsider their approach for the sequel. The result is [b]Far Cry 3[/b], a game that feels like less like an intentional piece of interactive art and more like a product designed to appeal to as many people as possible.

And it seems like Ubisoft was onto something, because the sales of Far Cry 3 exceeded more than three times those of part two. But the question is, does this scattershot approach to game design actually make for a better game?

The story follows Jason Brody, who, along with his two brothers and a group of friends, lands on the pirate-infested Rook Island during a skydiving trip. After being captured by the pirates and held for ransom, Jason escapes with the help of his older brother Grant, who's shot and killed in the process. Now it's up to Jason to nut up, pick up a gun, find and free his friends, and help the local Rakyat tribe get rid of the pirates once and for all.

It's been a minute since I last played Far Cry 3, and it's not like I was expecting much, but boy howdy, did I forget just how weak the story of this game is. The annoying characters and predictable plot beats are bad enough on their own, and the pretentious use of quotes from Lewis Carroll's Alice books between chapters certainly doesn't help things.

It continues the pattern of frustrating writing set up in the previous game, where you can just see the writers thinking they're being super clever and deep, completely oblivious to the fact their story has all the complexity and nuance of an 80s action flick. Hell, they even name the final mission "Hard Choice", despite it being one of the easiest choices I've ever made during my near three decades of gaming.

There's also the dubious use of the "white savior" cliché, one that Ubisoft wisely identified and avoided in future games. Apparently, the Rakyat warriors just didn't have it in them to rise up against the pirates, and it took a trust fund kid from Santa Monica to drop in and show them how it's done.

But none of this is really why we're here. After all, the story is just an excuse for shooting a bunch of bad guys, and on that front, Far Cry 3 is infinitely more palatable than Far Cry 2.

And I'm intentionally using "more palatable" and not "better", since both games are aiming for vastly different play experiences. If Far Cry 2 was trying to reinforce the narrative themes of brutality and misery through its gameplay, Far Cry 3 is doing the exact opposite. No more dodgy weapons, unwieldy map, or infinitely spawning enemy camps - any and all obstacles to the power fantasy the game is trying to build are gone.

As a result, all the gameplay conventions set up in part three would be carried over to the rest of the sequels as the patented Far Cry Formula™. Once you're through the opening bit, you're free to run around Rook Island engaging in a mix of story quests, side missions, unjamming com towers to reveal more of the map, and liberating enemy camps to lower the general threat level. There's also a ton of collectibles and side activities, the vast majority of which you can safely avoid, since they're just there to pad out the play time.

To help with all this, you get an entire arsenal of (mostly) modifiable weapons you can either buy for cold hard cash or unlock by climbing com towers. Notably, many of the weapons now come with sound suppressors, which makes sense given how much emphasis part three puts on stealth.

Overall, the gameplay is pretty smooth and enjoyable, allowing you approach objectives on your own terms by giving you plenty of combat and traversal options. It's clear that stealthy gameplay is the intended play experience - after all, you get 3x the XP if you liberate an outpost undetected - but on the default difficulty level, there's really nothing to stop you from Ramboing it up either.

The XP you earn during all the activities mentioned above can be used to unlock new takedown techniques and passive bonuses. This is represented in the form of Jason's ever-expanding tatau, which is supposed to bring him more in tune with the spirit of a tribal warrior.

The only real fly in the ointment is the fact that the side missions become really repetitive really fast. The two main flavors include killing enemy commanders with a knife or hunting animals with whatever weapon is provided at the mission checkpoint.

Speaking of hunting, the environments of Rook Island are teeming with fauna, most of which you'll have to decimate if you want to upgrade the, quite frankly, pitiful ammo capacity and number of weapon slots. As someone who doesn't enjoy violence against animals even when it comes to video games, the whole thing just felt really distasteful, especially considering you have to kill unique animals if you want the top-tier upgrades. It's not like this was a necessary part of the play experience either: Far Cry 5 takes place in rural USA, a part of the world famous for its fetishization of gun culture and sport hunting, and there you can just buy upgrades using cash.

The presentation is on point, rendering the dense foliage and varied biomes of Rook Island in a believable and immersive way. The voice acting is miles above anything in parts one and two, with a standout performance by Michael Mando. The music is serviceable, with one notable exception - can we please, [i]please[/i], retire the Ride of the Valkyries as the default helicopter action soundtrack?

In the end, Far Cry 3 works sort of like an amusement park ride: you're supposed to marvel at the pretty set pieces and enjoy the thrilling gameplay, but don't look for anything too deep here. If it grabs you as something you might want to try out, get it at a deep discount. And if you're not really sold, just play the (at least for me) superior part four.

Overall, I’d say Far Cry 3 is a solid game, but not quite as amazing as some people make it out to be.

The gunplay is fun and satisfying, and the stealth mechanics are actually really enjoyable – probably the highlight of the game for me. Clearing outposts in different ways never really gets old. The world itself is also nice to explore, even if it can feel a bit repetitive after a while.

That said, the story didn’t really click with me. I know a lot of people love it (especially Vaas), but personally I didn’t feel that connected to the characters, so it didn’t hit as hard as it could have.

Achievements weren’t too difficult overall, but the experience was a bit frustrating at times due to crashes, which made going for 100% more annoying than it should be.

In general, it’s a good game with strong gameplay, but the story and characters just weren’t really my type.

7/10

Far Cry 3 may be over thirteen years old, but it remains one of the most influential and enjoyable open-world shooters ever made. It isn't perfect, but its atmosphere, villain, and sense of freedom continue to stand out in an era filled with bigger yet less memorable games. If you've never played it, it's still worth experiencing. If you have, there's a good chance the Rook Islands will pull you back in one more time.

Not everything has aged perfectly. The mission structure can become repetitive, some side activities feel dated, and the second half of the story loses some momentum after its strongest moments.

Of course, no discussion of Far Cry 3 is complete without mentioning Vaas Montenegro. Even in 2026, he remains one of gaming's most recognizable villains.

A legendary masterpiece that defined open-world shooters forever!

Far Cry 3 is an absolute milestone in gaming history and a title I will never forget. The tropical Rook Islands are breathtaking, but what truly makes this game legendary is Vaas Montenegro. He is easily one of the greatest, most charismatic, and genuinely terrifying villains ever created. Every scene with him is unforgettable.

The gameplay loop is incredibly satisfying. Watching your character grow from a helpless, terrified tourist into a fierce, unstoppable warrior is masterfully executed through the tattoo progression system. Scouting enemy outposts, crafting gear from wild animal skins, and using stealth to clear camps feels perfect. And of course, the iconic weed-burning mission with Skrillex blasting in the background is pure gaming perfection. 

If you want an immersive story, chaotic sandbox freedom, and an unforgettable antagonist, Far Cry 3 is a mandatory playthrough for any gamer.

I LOVED this game. Great story and gameplay but its not without its faults, I did love the game but I have some things to discuss. First off Vaas, the villain who's on the cover of the game... isn't the main bad guy??? ok.. fine, ok vaas seems to mention there is a man far worse than him. Cool this could be interesting! A man named Hoyt, ok let's see just how bad he is, let's see what all that anticipation was for, he must be horrible!!! oh... he, he only gets like 3 minutes of screen time and kills only one guy... you have seen vaas do SO MUCH MORE THAN THAT. He was the most anticlimactic person I have EVER fought in a game. They talked so much about him just like House of a thousand corpses did with Dr. Satan, all the  hype just to be let down at the very end and all that hype having been for nothing. VAAS made this game, I assure you no one ever remembers Hoyt. Ok now that that's over with, My other issue was that all the boss fights is just in a empty black void, felt like a lack of effort and creativity. Nontheless I give this game a 8/10 until Vaas dies, then the game gets a 4/10.

Far Cry 3 remains a memorable FPS classic, with its atmospheric environments and detailed world design being the main reasons I recommend it. The story feels less compelling compared to the immersive setting, yet the game still draws players in through exploration and ambiance. On newer CPU/GPU hardware released around 2020, crashes can occur, which disrupts the experience and highlight compatibility issues. Even with these drawbacks, the environmental nuance and attention to detail make it worth playing for those who value atmosphere over narrative.

Crash-simulator.

The game itself is great, Ubisofts launcher is annoying, needing a third party launcher at all is annoying and having to put your password in every day you play is annoying too but these are sacrifices that I take for one of the best games ever made. But the crashes are a huge issue. You will find a lot of threads here on steam fully focusing on that specific issue but no one really has a concrete answer. I've tried mods, lowering graphics, chanign directX, starting from the steamfolder etc. - some work better than others but the game WILL crash. ON TOP OF THAT: Achievements randomly started to desync, meaning that I just didn't get them anymore for some time until I had to go out of my way to fix that too.

Still, I was able to complete the game all the way up to 100% (on low graphics at directX9) and I really had a blast. The game holds up incredibly well.

Man, Far Cry 3 is just an absolute masterpiece. I still find myself coming back to this game even years after it dropped, and it honestly holds up so well.

When you first start out, you play as Jason Brody—this totally normal, kind of spoiled tourist guy who gets captured by pirates while vacationing on a tropical island. The main villain, Vaas, is hands down one of the best bad guys in video game history. He is completely unhinged, terrifying, and stealing every single scene he’s in. The voice acting for him is just incredible.

What I love most is how the game handles Jason’s journey. You start off terrified, barely able to handle a gun, but as you explore the island, hunt wild animals to upgrade your gear, and take over pirate outposts, you watch him slowly turn into this absolute jungle warrior. It feels so satisfying because you are the one getting better at the game alongside him.

The open world is just a blast to play in. You can stealth your way into a camp with a bow and arrow, or you can do what I usually do: throw a piece of meat into the middle of the camp, watch a tiger run in and cause absolute chaos, and then clean up the rest with a shotgun. The freedom to tackle missions however you want never gets old.

If you haven’t played it yet, do yourself a favor and pick it up. It’s got a great story, awesome gunplay, a beautiful setting, and a villain you will never forget. Easily a 10/10 for me.

Game was fun. A few graphics glitching but I assume it was because of how old the game was. Also I had to download LAA software because like many old games. this game will crash every 15mins so keep that in mind if you plan on playing it now

Far Cry 3 -review
graphics- good, iconic for this great game
gameplay- very good, I enjoyed every second of it :D
audience- teens and adults
pc requirements- decent 
game size- ok, 11 GB
difficulty- easy to learn, (achievements are easy they just need time)
story- at least first half of it will change your life
game time- average, 41 hours for me (all achievements)
price-20 eur, it is ok but I recomend you to buy it when it is on sale for better price
bugs- only few, its understandable for an older game
?/10- 10/10 one of my favorite games of all time, with the best villain Vaas and unforgettable atmosphere, I really recommend this game !!

Far Cry 3 was the game that made me fall in love with the series. The island felt dangerous and alive, Vaas is still one of the greatest villains in gaming, and the progression from surviving to becoming an unstoppable force felt incredibly satisfying. Absolute classic.
Chaotic gameplay mixed with a very good story and an unforgettable villain. Yeah, I'd recommend this game to anyone really. Far Cry 3 is great!
